_meta.purpose: Curated bedrock list of plants characteristic of Solent saltmarsh — independent of iNaturalist coverage. Picker can draw from this pool any time of year regardless of whether anyone has recently logged them, since these species ARE the saltmarsh by definition.
_meta.reference: Rodwell, J.S. (1995) British Plant Communities Vol 5: Maritime communities. UK National Vegetation Classification saltmarsh communities SM1–SM28.
_meta.habitatBackstop: JNCC UK BAP Priority Habitat: Coastal Saltmarsh — https://jncc.gov.uk/our-work/uk-bap-priority-habitats/
_meta.monthsAre: 1=January .. 12=December. 'flowering' = months in flower; 'visible' = months the plant is recognisable in the marsh (perennials are mostly all year).
_meta.zones: lower-marsh (pioneer / regularly tidally inundated), mid-marsh (twice-daily flooded), upper-marsh (only flooded by spring/storm tides), strandline (drift line above HW)
_meta.linksNote: Wikipedia URLs are predictable from Genus_species; iNat URLs are search-based. BSBI Plant Atlas URLs use scrambled IDs — to add per-species, fetch from https://plantatlas2020.org or via https://bsbi.org/taxa search.

notes: Salt-adapted annual of bare mud near the lowest tides — bright green in summer, glowing red after autumn frosts before dying back to reveal the marsh's bare bones in winter.

The cord-grass that defines the lower-marsh edge — coarse tussocks of stiff grey-green leaves. The hybrid arose right here in Southampton Water in the 1870s and was deliberately spread worldwide for coastal stabilisation; in retreat since the 1950s die-back.
Soft fleshy mat-former among the glasswort — green for most of summer, turning crimson and purple in autumn alongside the rest of the lower marsh.
The dominant grass of the mid-marsh sward — a tough, salt-tolerant turf that forms the floor of a healthy marsh and binds the surface against scour.
Silvery, mat-forming sub-shrub of mid-marsh creek edges — lines the channel banks so densely that the saltings can look silver-grey across the middle of summer.
Lays a mauve haze across the mid-marsh in July and August. Not actually a lavender — flowers are tiny but the cumulative purple wash is the colour of high summer here.
Mauve daisy of mid-marsh, flowering July to October — often the last colour of the marsh year, holding on after the sea-lavender has gone over. Older books call it Aster tripolium.
Tough, fleshy-leaved plantain of mid and upper marsh — narrow ribbed leaves in a basal rosette, a small green spike of flowers in summer.
Slender spike of small green flowers above arrow-narrow leaves — easy to miss in the sward unless you're looking down through it.
Tiny pink five-petalled flowers among the mid-marsh grasses, open only in sun — close to invisible on a dull day.
Low creeper of mid to upper marsh with small pink-and-white flowers in the leaf-axils — overlooked between the tussocks. Older books call it Glaux maritima.
One of the first plants to flower on the marsh — small white four-petalled flowers from April. Sailors used to chew the leaves against scurvy on the long voyages out of Southampton.
Tall, sharp-pointed rush forming dark green stands at the upper-marsh edge — its zone marks roughly where most spring tides reach.
Coarse blue-green grass marking the highest reach of the tide — the boundary between marsh and dry land. Often abbreviated 'sea couch' in older floras; some books still use Elymus athericus.
The wild ancestor of beetroot, growing along the drift line — glossy dark green leaves and a long spike of small green flowers in summer.
Sprawling annual of the strandline among tide-cast wrack — green to bronze with distinctive arrow-shaped leaves.
